texts.orig: 1. Introduction
texts.text: 1. Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The capture and storage of carbon dioxide (CO2) from combustion flue gases (post-combustion capture, PCC) is now recognised by government and industry as a viable near-term option for greenhouse gas abatement (IEA, 2007; IPCC, 2005). It is particularly relevant to electricity generation from fossil fuels (coal, oil and gas) which accounts for approximately 25% of global CO2 emissions (WRI, 2007) with this figure set to increase drastically in the next 25 years (CSIRO, 2006). PCC has a number of practical advantages over other methods such as oxy-firing and pre-combustion capture and is of similar economic cost (Davison, 2007). In particular, PCC can be retro-fitted to existing power stations and integrated into new ones. Additionally, the parasitic energy demand of a PCC plant on a power station can be reduced (at the cost of reduced CO2 removal) according to electricity demand such as when additional output is required from a power station during times of peak load or optimal electricity pricing.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Vapour-liquid-equilibria (VLE) models of CO2-amine-H2O systems are critical to understand the behaviour of these systems as a function of composition and temperature. Such models are a crucial component of any process simulation and the prediction of process performance. VLE models consist of chemical reactions and an activity model to describe non-ideal chemical behaviour at high ionic strength. For CO2-amine-H2O systems complex and highly dimensional activity models are used such as Pitzer, UNIQUAC and eNRTL equations. These require many tens of parameters to be known or determined, and in many cases this leads to over-fitting. The resulting parameter values then have little physical meaning and extrapolation to different conditions is unreliable. In this work a much simpler activity model, specific ion interaction theory (SIT), has been used and its performance assessed using published VLE data of CO2 absorption in aqueous MEA, AMP, PZ and mixtures of AMP and PZ. This simple activity model, which requires the determination of few if any parameters, coupled to an appropriate chemical model, was found to yield results as good as or better than that found for the more complex models. Extrapolation using this simpler model will be much more reliable than a complex model that contains many ill-defined parameter values and its inclusion in process simulation is much more straightforward.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: ammonia production industries (IEA, 2004). Traditionally, the CO2 is vented or used for enhanced oil recovery, food manufacturing or chemicals production (IEA, 2004). The most mature and applied technology for the capture and release of CO2 is cyclic chemical absorption/stripping using an aqueous amine solution of 30% (w/w) monoethanolamine (4.9 M MEA). Aqueous solutions of other amines that have better performance characteristics than MEA are also available as proprietary formulations from a number of commercial suppliers (e.g. Fluor, BASF and MHI). In a packed absorber column flue gas (entering from the bottom) and amine solution (entering from the top) are counter-currently contacted at atmospheric pressure and low temperature ( ∼ 40 ◦ C). The CO2 rich amine solution is then circulated to the top of a stripping column which is at high temperature ( ∼ 100-150 ◦ C). At this temperature and under the influence of stripping steam the chemical equilibria are shifted such that the absorbed CO2 is released and exits at the top of the column. The regenerated amine solution is then circulated back to the absorber column.

texts.orig: of CO2-amine-water systems as a function of composition and temperature. This VLE system includes the chemical equilibria in solution which define the solubility of CO2 in the absorbent. Due to the large concentrations used and associated high ionic strengths it is expected that the chemical equilibria will behave non-ideally due to changes in activity of the chemical species. Typically this nonideal behaviour has been described using complex Pitzer (Bottinger et al., 2008), UNIQUAC (Aronu et al., 2011) and eNRTL (Zhang and Chen, 2011) type models based on the assumption that simpler activity models are inadequate. This assumption has not been tested. Using these complex activity models requires many tens of parameters to be known beforehand or determined. These are often regressed against relatively small experimental datasets resulting in parameter over-fitting in which many of the parameters are poorly defined and of little physical relevance. Such over-fitting can be avoided through the application of techniques such as regularisation, or cross validation, which give insight into the sensitivity and robustness of the determined parameters, but this is generally not done. Furthermore, this over-fitting means such models cannot be reliably extrapolated which poses a problem for their inclusion in process models.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: However there are some previous publications that suggest such complexity may not be required to represent VLE data. For example semi-empirical algebraic equations derived from the overall reaction chemistry have been shown to provide good fits to VLE data using as few as three parameters (Gabrielsen, 2007; Gabrielsen et al., 2005; Posey et al., 1996). However this approach lacks the ability to determine chemical speciation or predict the behaviour of the VLE systems outside the range of conditions used for parameter determination. Another approach that requires relatively few parameters is based on the method of Kent and Eisenberg (Aroua and Salleh, 2004; Bishnoi, 2000; Dang, 2000; Kent and Eisenberg, 1976; Posey, 1996) in which the equilibrium constants describing a series of relevant reactions and the mass and/or charge balance are arranged into a polynomial. Also the temperature dependence of the equilibrium constants is described using polynomials. This is not dissimilar to the approach used here but speciation is typically not determined (although it can be). Also any non-ideal behaviour will be captured in the polynomial parameters, limiting the ability to extrapolate outside the conditions of parameter determination.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this work the assumption has been tested that complex activity models are required to describe VLE data when using straightforward chemical equations. It was found that a much simpler model based on specific ion interaction theory (SIT) (Guggenheim and Turgeon, 1955) was adequate to describe VLE data of aqueous MEA, 2-amino-2-methyl-propanol (AMP), piperazine (PZ), and AMP-PZ mixtures at high concentrations. In most cases the agreement between the model and measured data was equivalent to or better than the more complex models. The parameters of the chemical and activity model used can be straightforwardly determined from a minimum of laboratory experiments, making it a much more tractable and reliable approach for the inclusion of new absorbent chemistry in process models.

texts.orig: The absorption of CO2 into an aqueous amine solution can be divided into two separate groups of reactivity's. The reactions of CO2 with water and the subsequent carbonate acid-base equilibria, and the reactions of CO2 with amines and the amine acid-base equilibria. In an aqueous amine absorbent there is interplay between these chemistries and a fully descriptive chemical model must include both.

texts.orig: Eq. (4) is amine protonation and Eq. (5) represents carbamate formation. For monoamines protonation of the carbamate species may occur and the p Ka is around 7 for MEA (Fernandes et al., 2012a). This reaction was included for MEA as it may contribute to the chemistry at high CO2 loadings. The p Ka for the AMP carbamate is not known but it can be considered irrelevant as the AMP-carbamate species forms to only a minor extent. For diamines like PZ the formation of the dicarbamate needs to be included, Eq. (7). Protonation of the mono-carbamate species occurs typically with a higher p Ka than monoamines (Fernandes et al., 2012a) and this reaction needs to be included as Eq. (6). Dicarbamate formation involves a number of reactions with a number of forms of mono and dicarbamate species existing in equilibrium (Conway et al., 2013). Eq. (7) represents the dicarbamate formation equilibrium that is relevant at PCC conditions. The p Ka for protonation of the dicarbamate species has been determined to be 7.99 (Conway et al., 2013) so its inclusion is also necessary (Eq. (8)).

(7) represents the dicarbamate formation equilibrium that is relevant at PCC conditions. The p Ka for protonation of the dicarbamate species has been determined to be 7.99 (Conway et al., 2013) so its inclusion is also necessary (Eq. (8)).

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.3. Activity coefficients
texts.text: 2.3. Activity coefficients

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Many different approaches are available for the estimation of activity coefficients in an attempt to account for the influence of ionic interactions on the effective concentration of chemical species. There are semi-emperical approaches such as the Davies equation (Davies, 1962), approaches with a more complete derivation from theory such as the Debye-Hückel equation (Debye and Hückel, 1923), and extensive and complex systems such as Pitzer equations (Pitzer, 1995) which are applicable even in saturated salt solutions. There are many adaptations and extensions of these methods. However, what is often not asked is what is the simplest activity model available that adequately represents the data? Between the years 2000 and 2005 the IUPAC project 'Ionic Strength Corrections for Stability Constants' (IUPAC project number 2000003-1-500) attempted to address this question. Their conclusion was that for most applications specific ion interaction theory (SIT) (Guggenheim and Turgeon, 1955) was the method of choice for ionic strengths up to 6 mol dm -3 . The form of SIT as used here is given in Eq. (9) and has been modified to be in mol dm -3 rather than mole fraction. It consists of the extended Debye-Hückel equation with the product of the constant B and ionic radius å replaced with a constant of value 1.5. The equation also includes interaction parameters ε that are only relevant ( / = 0) for interactions between ions of opposite charge. SIT was chosen for this work due to the recommendation from IUPAC and for its simplicity of implementation.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The chemical model for MEA, a primary monoamine, consisted of equations Eqs. (1)-(6). That is CO2-H2O chemistry, MEA protonation and formation of the MEA carbamate species and protonation of the carbamate. The VLE data from (Aronu et al., 2011) was chosen as it represents a large and recent data set covering a broad range of MEA concentrations, CO2 loadings and temperatures. In total there are 144 data points covering 2.5-10 mol dm -3 MEA, CO2 loadings up to 0.6 and temperatures from 313 to 393 K. Thus any chemical model that adequately represents this data set well can be considered to cover all chemical and physical behaviour relevant to a CO2 absorption process.

texts.orig: In the work of (Aronu et al., 2011) the data were regressed using the extended UNIQUAC thermodynamic model framework. This is an extensive thermodynamic model based on chemical potential and includes temperature dependent interaction parameters as part of the calculation of activity coefficients. A total of 30 parameters were regressed against 144 data points. The model contains considerably more than these 30 regressed parameters that were set to published values. The resulting AARD% from this regression was 24.3% compared to 15.4% from the regression of six parameters using the model described here. Thus an increasing of the model complexity beyond that given here is over-fitting. The consequence of over-fitting is that the determined parameter values are of no physical significance, and while the model may represent the dataset well, any extrapolation will result in highly unreliable values.

However, the resulting parameter values are different (e.g. 9.68 instead of 9.22 for amine protonation). This suggests that the changes in parameter values are able to compensate for the lack of activity correction. texts.text: 3.2. 2-Amino-2-methyl-1-propanol (AMP)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Like MEA, AMP is also a primary monoamine. It has two additional methyl groups attached to the carbon in the 𝛂 -position relative the nitrogen. The consequence of this is steric hindrance and electronic effects that destabilise the formation of a carbamate and increase the basicity of AMP (Fernandes et al., 2012b; Sartori and Savage, 1983). VLE data for AMP was chosen from two recent sources, (Kundu et al., 2003) and (Tong et al., 2012). These combined sources correspond to 80 data points covering AMP concentrations of 2-3.4 mol dm -3 , CO2 loadings up to 0.97 and temperatures of 303-373 K.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: If the mean of the published values for AMP protonation and carbamate formation given in Table 3 are used the AARD% is 17.6%. The results of regressing the equilibrium constants and enthalpies of Eqs. (4) and (5) against this data are also given in Table 3. The regressed values for the protonation constant and the enthalpy of protonation, lgK 0 4 and /triangle H 4 , are in good agreement with published values and the overall AARD% is 14.2%. The agreement for carbamate formation equilibrium constant is also good but there is large uncertainty associated with the equilibrium enthalpy (100%) and it is essentially not defined. The uncertainty associated with carbamate formation is consistent with the fact that AMP is known to only form a weakly stable carbamate (Sartori and Savage, 1983; Silkenbaumer et al., 1998), if at all (Fernandes et al., 2012a). Carbamate formation contributes little to the net CO2 absorption. VLE data is a bulk measure of CO2 absorbed and is relatively insensitive to species that form in small amounts. As a consequence the temperature dependence of AMP carbamate formation is poorly defined by VLE data. Further, there is no discerable amount of protonation of the carbamate and thus this equilibrium and its constants has not been included.

texts.orig: The interaction of the two amines means that some reactions may not occur to a significant extent. In this case, the fact that AMP is present in excess and has a p Ka similar to PZ means that much less PZ will be protonated than in a PZ only solution. Also the presence of PZ means that formation of AMP carbamate is unlikely to make a significant contribution to net absorption due to its low stability relative to the PZ carbamate. Regressions were carried out including all possible reactions and it was found that AMP carbamate formation, PZ protonation and PZ monocarbamate protonation were poorly defined by the data and could be excluded. The equilibrium constants and enthalpies for these reactions were fixed to the previously regressed values. Alternatively the formation of the dicarbamate PZ species was well defined by this dataset.

texts.orig: If no regression was carried out against the AMP and PZ data and only the parameter values from the previous regression were used an AARD% of 55.6% results with the parity plot shown in Fig. 5. The main contribution to the increased AARD% when using the previously regressed values is that when PZ was considered in isolation the parameters for formation of the dicarbamate species were poorly defined. Based on the calculated speciation this is because when dicarbamate formation could be expected the pH is such that it does not form to an appreciable extent due to the formation of the protonated monocarbamate species. However, in the presence of AMP, which acts as a pH buffer, dicarbamate formation becomes much more significant. In the AMP and PZ mixtures AMPH + and HCO3 -are the most prevalent species. The conditions are such that the dominant PZ species is PZ(COO)2 2 -at most loadings considered. Thus, if the badly defined and incorrect value for Ig K 0 7 from the PZ-only data is used instead of the now well defined and relevant value, the quality of the prediction clearly suffers significantly. This is illustrated in Fig. 6 which shows the calculated speciation for 308 K and AMP and PZ concentrations of 2.81 and 0.58 mol dm -3 respectively. This represents mid-range conditions from the dataset. PZCOO -is significant at low loading but most of the data is above a loading of 0.5, at which point its concentration is depleted. This explains why the enthalpy associated with this reaction is not defined as there is only a small amount of data at loadings below 0.5, and this data covers only a subset of the temperature range measured. Alternatively the concentration of HOOCPZCOO -increases at high loading. This dominance of AMPH + , PZ(COO)2 2 -and HOOCPZCOO -explains why the parameters for formation of these species are well defined by this dataset while those for monocarbamate formation, monocarbamate protonation and PZ protonation are not.

texts.orig: 4. Conclusions
texts.text: 4. Conclusions

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In the work it has been demonstrated that vapour-liquidequilibria data for the aqueous amine and CO2 systems of MEA, AMP, PZ and AMP-PZ mixtures can be modelled using a simple system of chemical reactions with a simple activity model. This is in contrast with the wide-spread and apparently accepted doctrine which assumes complex and highly dimensional activity models based around Pitzer equations, or similar, are required.

texts.orig: well-founded theory. While Pitzer equations and others of similar higher complexity have a better theoretical foundation and are certainly a requirement for many situations involving highly concentrated solutions, for the purpose of PCC modelling they are not superior as too many parameters are simply not determinable. The number of parameters means that a good agreement between the model and the data will result, however the usefulness of these parameters for prediction or extrapolation is limited by this overfitting. To reliably determine the parameters of these complex activity models requires substantial experimental effort and analysis. In particular a careful sensitivity analysis of the model system is essential.

To reliably determine the parameters of these complex activity models requires substantial experimental effort and analysis. In particular a careful sensitivity analysis of the model system is essential.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The main advantages of a simpler activity model that adequately represents the non-idealities in a physically meaningful are twofold: the model parameters will be well defined by the data; and once determined should be reusable across a range of conditions and compositions. This has been demonstrated to be the case for the SIT based approach in this work. This then provides a far simpler model framework for inclusion in process modelling and performance prediction tasks.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A remaining question is if the same type of simple SIT based activity model can also be used to describe non-ideal kinetic behaviour in CO2-amine-H2O systems. Non-idealities influence the effective concentration of species in solution and thus impact on reaction kinetics as well as the thermodynamics. Correct account of this non-ideal behaviour should allow the determination of reaction rate constants independent of composition in analogy to the determination of equilibrium constants at zero ionic strength. The suitability of a SIT based activity model for this is currently being investigated.
